headline: From Grief to Action: How One Researcher Built Mental Health Support for African Youth
meta_description: A 26-year-old PhD researcher at Cambridge turned personal tragedy into the Ndinewe Foundation, bringing culturally relevant mental health resources to young Af…
summary_short: After witnessing a tragedy that exposed a critical gap in youth mental health support, a Zimbabwean researcher founded an organization providing peer support and educati…
body:
When a personal tragedy revealed how many young people in Zimbabwe had nowhere to turn for mental health support, one researcher decided to act.

Today, 26-year-old Ndlela Chikaura leads the Ndinewe Foundation—a Zimbabwean organization whose name means "I am with you" in English. From her position as a PhD researcher at the University of Cambridge, Chikaura has built a platform delivering locally and culturally relevant mental health resources, peer support, and education to young people across her country.

"I saw the gap. I saw the need," she recalled of that pivotal moment. The foundation's approach is distinctly youth-centered: young people themselves help design solutions rather than simply receiving top-down interventions.

One innovative program integrates mental health education into sporting activities for children and adolescents. This intersection of sport and mental wellness will be featured at the UN's upcoming "One World, One Game, One Goal" event, where Chikaura joins 14 other Young Leaders for the Sustainable Development Goals.

The urgency is stark. According to the World Health Organization, suicide ranks as the third leading cause of death among people aged 15 to 29, with most occurring in low- and middle-income countries. Despite this crisis, mental health receives less than two percent of many national health budgets.

"Every community deserves access to mental health support," Chikaura emphasized. "No young person should be prevented from reaching their potential because of untreated challenges."
